questions.q: A coalition government is one that is formed by:
options: A single party with a clear majority
questions.explanation: A coalition government is formed when two or more parties come together to command a majority.

questions.q: The era of frequent coalition governments at the national level is generally said to have begun from the:
questions.explanation: Coalition politics became a recurring feature of national government from the late 1980s onward.

questions.q: Coalition governments tend to form when no single party wins a majority in the:
questions.explanation: Coalitions arise when no single party secures a majority of seats in the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: Which of the following is an alliance associated with the coalition era?
questions.explanation: The National Front was one of the alliances that emerged during the coalition era.

questions.q: The abbreviation NDA in Indian politics stands for the:
questions.explanation: In the context of coalitions, NDA refers to the National Democratic Alliance.

questions.q: The abbreviation UPA in Indian politics stands for the:
questions.explanation: UPA refers to the United Progressive Alliance, a coalition grouping.

questions.q: In a coalition, parties that support the government but do not join the cabinet are said to give:
questions.explanation: Parties supporting a government without joining it are said to provide outside support.

questions.q: A written statement of agreed policies among coalition partners is commonly called a:
questions.explanation: Coalition partners often agree on a Common Minimum Programme setting out shared policy goals.

questions.q: Which of these is the largest single party in a coalition usually called?
questions.explanation: The largest party in a coalition typically acts as the lead or anchor party of the alliance.

questions.q: The alliances National Front, United Front, NDA and UPA are all examples of:
questions.explanation: These are all political coalitions or fronts formed during the era of coalition governments.

questions.q: The National Front government that took office in 1989 was led by which Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: V.P. Singh headed the National Front coalition government formed after the 1989 election.

questions.q: The United Front governments of 1996-1998 were led by which two Prime Ministers in succession?
options: Rajiv Gandhi and P.V. Narasimha Rao
H.D. Deve Gowda and I.K. Gujral
questions.explanation: H.D. Deve Gowda and then I.K. Gujral led the United Front coalition governments between 1996 and 1998.

questions.q: The first full five-year term completed by a coalition government at the Union level was that of which alliance from 1999 to 2004?
questions.explanation: The NDA government completed a full term from 1999 to 2004, the first coalition to do so.

questions.q: The United Progressive Alliance formed the Union government following which general elections?
questions.explanation: The UPA formed the government after the 2004 and 2009 general elections.

questions.q: Which Prime Minister headed the NDA government during its full term from 1999 to 2004?
questions.explanation: Atal Bihari Vajpayee was Prime Minister during the NDA's full term from 1999 to 2004.

questions.q: Which Prime Minister led the UPA coalition governments from 2004 to 2014?
questions.explanation: Manmohan Singh was Prime Minister during the UPA's two terms from 2004 to 2014.

questions.q: Coalition governments are particularly associated with the working of which feature of Indian democracy?
questions.explanation: Coalition politics reflects India's multi-party system, in which many parties contest and hold seats.

questions.q: A law of 1985 intended to bring stability to governments, including coalitions, by curbing party-switching is the:
questions.explanation: The Anti-Defection Law of 1985 sought to curb defections and lend stability to governments.

questions.q: When a coalition loses its majority, the test the government may face on the floor of the Lok Sabha is a:
questions.explanation: A coalition that loses majority support may fall on a no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: In appointing a Prime Minister during a hung Parliament, the constitutional figure who plays a central role is the:
questions.explanation: The President invites a leader who can command the confidence of the Lok Sabha to form the government.

questions.q: A Lok Sabha in which no single party has a majority is commonly described as a:
questions.explanation: A Lok Sabha with no single-party majority is referred to as a hung Parliament, often producing coalitions.

questions.q: Coalition governments at the Union level frequently rely on the support of:
questions.explanation: National coalitions have often depended on the support of regional and state-based parties.

questions.q: The Chandra Shekhar government of 1990-91 is notable in the coalition era because it governed with outside support from the:
questions.explanation: Chandra Shekhar's breakaway faction formed a government with outside support from the Congress in 1990-91.

questions.q: The 1996 government formed immediately after that year's election lasted only about thirteen days before resigning; its Prime Minister was:
questions.explanation: Atal Bihari Vajpayee's first government in 1996 lasted about thirteen days before resigning for want of a majority.

questions.q: The 1979 government led by Charan Singh, an early instance of fragile coalition arithmetic, is notable because he:
options: Resigned without facing the Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: Charan Singh's government resigned before facing the Lok Sabha, an early example of coalition instability.

questions.q: The Janata Party government of 1977, often seen as a forerunner of later coalitions, was formed by the merger of several parties opposed to the:
questions.explanation: The Janata Party of 1977 was formed by the coming together of parties opposed to the Congress, foreshadowing later coalitions.

questions.q: A landmark constitutional pronouncement that the strength of a government should be tested on the floor of the House came in which 1994 case?
options: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
questions.explanation: The S.R. Bommai judgment of 1994 affirmed that majority should be tested on the floor of the House, important in coalition politics.

questions.q: The general election of 2014 marked a change from the immediately preceding coalition pattern because it produced:
options: A government with no Prime Minister
A government formed by the President
A single party with a Lok Sabha majority leading the NDA
questions.explanation: The 2014 election returned a single party with a Lok Sabha majority that led the NDA, after years of coalition-dependent governments.

questions.q: During coalition years, the President's role in inviting a leader to form a government draws on which constitutional requirement?
options: That the Council of Ministers commands the confidence of the Lok Sabha
That the Prime Minister be from the largest state
That the Rajya Sabha approve the Prime Minister
questions.explanation: A government must command the confidence of the Lok Sabha, which guides the President's choice in coalition situations.

questions.q: The National Front, United Front, NDA and UPA are best understood as:
options: Pre- or post-poll groupings of multiple parties
questions.explanation: These were groupings of several parties, formed before or after elections, rather than single parties.
